exercises.title: A: Multiple Choice (Podcasting text)
exercises.instructions: Choose the correct answer (A, B, C or D) for each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Choose the correct answer (A, B, C or D) for each gap.
exercises.passage: Before podcasting went mainstream, it was (1) ...... for granted that audio content could only be produced by trained professionals with expensive studio equipment. In other (2) ...... , listeners were simply passive recipients of whatever topics producers chose to broadcast. The concept has certainly (3) ...... on and, for tens of millions of users, independent audio shows now (4) ...... a real appetite for niche, in-depth storytelling. Leading podcast networks (5) ...... that the format encourages deeper engagement than traditional radio because listeners actively choose every episode they consume. Despite this, media watchdogs have (6) ...... several popular shows for spreading health misinformation without editorial oversight. Certain programmes have indeed (7) ...... unverified claims as established medical or scientific fact. However, although these incidents have received wide media (8) ...... , they arguably (9) ...... the extent of misinformation that existed in older broadcast formats. Most listeners have little (10) ...... of how much unverified content circulated freely on conventional radio long before digital alternatives emerged.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is fixed phrase, so the correct answer is "taken".

wrongOptions.A: "given" does not form the natural collocation needed here — "taken" fits better.
wrongOptions.given: "given" does not form the natural collocation needed here — "taken" fits better.
wrongOptions.C: "made" does not form the natural collocation needed here — "taken" fits better.
wrongOptions.made: "made" does not form the natural collocation needed here — "taken" fits better.
wrongOptions.D: "put" does not form the natural collocation needed here — "taken" fits better.
wrongOptions.put: "put" does not form the natural collocation needed here — "taken" fits better.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the words before and after the gap together — collocations are heard as a single chunk.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Fixed phrases must be learned as whole chunks — individual words may not translate literally.

explanations: Meet a need → **answers** a real appetite.
Slight awareness → little **inkling** of how much.

exercises.title: B: Key Word Transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.

items.sentence:
My boss is extremely displeased with me because I arrived late three days in a row. **books**
I ................................................................. because I arrived late three days in a row.
items.answer: am in my boss's bad books
acceptedAnswers: am in my boss's bad books
items.explanation: In trouble with boss → **am in my boss's bad books**.

items.sentence:
Nobody needs to be told that first impressions at a job interview matter enormously. **saying**
It ................................................................. that first impressions at a job interview matter enormously.

She hasn't confirmed anything, but my interpretation of the signs is that the director is planning to leave the company. **lines**
She hasn't confirmed anything, but ................................................................. , I'd say the director is planning to leave the company.

Alex and Daniel haven't spoken to each other since the argument last month. **terms**
Alex and Daniel are no longer ................................................................. since the argument last month.
items.explanation: Not speaking → no longer **on speaking terms**.

items.sentence:
It was wrong of you to persuade Marco to keep quiet about the incident. **talked**
You should ................................................................. about the incident.
items.answer: not have talked Marco into keeping quiet
acceptedAnswers: not have talked Marco into keeping quiet
items.explanation: Shouldn't have persuaded → **not have talked Marco into keeping quiet**.

items.sentence:
I'm certain the manager spoke to Claire about the complaints before the meeting. **word**
The manager ................................................................. the complaints before the meeting.
items.answer: must have had a word with Claire about
acceptedAnswers: must have had a word with Claire about
items.explanation: Certain past deduction → **must have had a word with Claire about**.

items.sentence:
I don't think Patrick misunderstood what you said, Sophie. **stick**
Patrick can't ................................................................., Sophie.
items.answer: have got the wrong end of the stick
acceptedAnswers: have got the wrong end of the stick
items.explanation: Misunderstood → **have got the wrong end of the stick**.

items.sentence:
There's no need for you to make any promises – I trust you completely. **word**
You ................................................................. – I trust you completely.
items.explanation: No promise needed → **needn't give me your word**.

exercises.title: C: Word Formation
exercises.instructions: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals.

items.sentence: Under his inspiring ...... (EDIT) , the university newspaper won several national awards.
Most of what she told the police was pure ...... (HEAR) – none of the neighbours had actually witnessed the event.
The closure of the mine will have widespread ...... (IMPLY) for employment across the entire county.
Describing that concert as 'quite enjoyable' is a bit of a/an ...... (STATE), given how extraordinary the performance was.
She's not a particularly ...... (TALK) child; she much prefers reading to chatting.
There's a/an ...... (WRITE) agreement among the research team never to discuss ongoing projects at social events.
In informal digital communication, people frequently overuse the ...... (EXCLAIM) mark to convey enthusiasm.
Candidates are ...... (EXPRESS) prohibited from accessing any online resources during the written assessment.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: D: One-word gap fill (phrasal verbs / expressions)
exercises.title: D: One-word gap fill (phrasal verbs / expressions)
exercises.instructions: Write one word in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: When does the new episode of that drama series come ...... on the streaming platform?
items.explanation: Be released → come **out** on the platform.

items.sentence: My sister was initially against the idea, but we finally managed to talk her ...... after showing her all the evidence.
I can't approve your request right now – I'll need to talk it ...... with the board of directors first.
items.explanation: Discuss → talk it **over** with the board.

items.sentence: Whatever you do, don't let ...... to Hannah that we're organising a surprise party for her.
items.explanation: Reveal accidentally → don't let **slip** to Hannah.

items.sentence: Jake accidentally blurted ...... in front of everyone that Mia had applied for the director's position.
I've memorised all my notes for the presentation, but I'm terrified I'll dry ...... the moment I stand in front of the board.
Someone needs to speak ...... against the proposed cuts to the school arts programme.
The chairwoman was shouted ...... by protesters the moment she tried to open the meeting.

items.sentenceAfter: that novel last year – it wasn't published until this January.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "can't have read" — impossible past.

wrongOptions.B: "couldn't read" is the wrong tense for a finished past time in this sentence.
wrongOptions.couldn't read: "couldn't read" is the wrong tense for a finished past time in this sentence.
wrongOptions.C: "mustn't have read" is the wrong tense for a finished past time in this sentence.
wrongOptions.mustn't have read: "mustn't have read" is the wrong tense for a finished past time in this sentence.
wrongOptions.D: "didn't need to read" is the wrong tense for a finished past time in this sentence.
wrongOptions.didn't need to read: "didn't need to read" is the wrong tense for a finished past time in this sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: When you see a finished past-time expression, reach for the past simple first.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: A finished past time marker (yesterday, last week, ago) requires the past simple, not a perfect tense.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Time expressions like for, since, already, and yet are strong present-perfect signals.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links past actions to now — look for markers like for, since, already, yet, or ever.
